data.title: Foreign Investors Buy $720.1B in US Stocks, Up 134% in 2025
data.description:
Global investors are voting with capital, not headlines. Despite President Donald Trump's tariffs rattling markets and reviving Sell America chatter, foreign buyers more than doubled their exposure to US equities in 2025. According to US Treasury Department data released Wednesday, non-US investors purchased a net $720.1 billion of American stocks last year, up 134% from $307.5 billion in 2024. That scale of buying suggests the narrative around capital flight may have been overstated, at least when it comes to equities.

Owen Lamont, senior vice-president and portfolio manager at Acadian Asset Management LLC, framed it bluntly: the Sell America story does not square with the data. In his view, what persists is a form of American exceptionalism, particularly the continued appetite for US technology shares. Even as tariff uncertainty drove volatility and raised concerns about Federal Reserve independence, enthusiasm around artificial intelligence was, until very recently, widely seen as a force that could lift corporate profitability. Still, US returns did not dominate globally. The S&P 500 Index (SPY) gained 16% for the year, trailing the MSCI World Excluding United States Index by 13 percentage points.

Drilling into the flows adds another layer. Excluding fund hubs such as the Cayman Islands and Ireland, Norway was the largest net buyer in 2025, purchasing $81.8 billion of US stocks, nearly triple its 2024 total, according to Bloomberg News analysis of data going back to 2023. Singapore followed with $79 billion after leading in 2024, while South Korea added $73.6 billion, almost five times more than the year before. Mainland China was a net seller for a third consecutive year, shedding $34.1 billion, with only Kuwait selling more at $36.5 billion. Canada, which had been the biggest seller in 2024, flipped to a net buyer with $10.6 billion in purchases, despite domestic anger over trade policies and concerns about sovereignty, a reversal that underscores how compelling US equities have remained for global allocators.

data.title: Augusta Precious Metals Review 2026 Analysis Released in Latest Industry Report
data.description:
New York, New York--(Newsfile Corp. - February 19, 2026) - IRAEmpire, a trusted independent source for financial insights and retirement-industry research, today announced the release of its 2026 Precious Metals Investment Report, featuring an in-depth review of Augusta Precious Metals, one of the nation's most established gold and silver IRA providers. The report highlights Augusta Precious Metals' performance, customer satisfaction ratings, and overall impact on the expanding precious metals investment landscape.

Read the Full Augusta Precious Metals Review Here.

The new analysis was developed after a comprehensive evaluation of major U.S. precious metals firms. Companies were assessed based on credibility, regulatory standing, fee transparency, customer experience, product diversity, storage partnerships, and long-term industry stability. Augusta Precious Metals' strong scores across these categories contributed to its position as one of the leading choices for Americans seeking physical assets and IRA diversification.

"Macroeconomic trends such as elevated central bank gold accumulation, real interest rate volatility, and constrained global mine supply have continued to influence investor interest in precious metals markets entering 2026," said the chief editor of IRAEmpire, Ryan Paulson.

"Precious metals have become an increasingly important part of retirement strategies as investors look for stability during market uncertainty," said an IRAEmpire spokesperson. "Our 2026 review of Augusta Precious Metals helps consumers navigate a crowded marketplace with clarity, providing a transparent look at one of the most well-known names in gold and silver IRAs."

Explore the Detailed 2026 Augusta Precious Metals Review

The report outlines the strengths that continue to set Augusta Precious Metals apart, including its industry experience, highly rated customer support, and education-first approach to metals investing. It also notes the company's transparent pricing structure, streamlined IRA rollover process, and ability to guide first-time investors through complex retirement planning decisions.

In addition to gold and silver IRAs, Augusta Precious Metals offers direct bullion purchases, one-on-one web conferences with account specialists, and secure storage options through trusted vaulting partners. These services contribute to the firm's long-standing reputation in the industry and growing popularity among investors seeking inflation hedges or portfolio diversification.

The full 2026 Augusta Precious Metals Review is now available on IRAEmpire's official website. The profile includes detailed insights on the company's offerings, pros and cons, service structure, and its ongoing role in strengthening the U.S. precious metals investment market.

Contact:

ryan@iraempire.com

Chief Editor, IRAEmpire

data.title: White House Talks Over Stalled Crypto Bill Drag on. It Matters for Coinbase. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Joe Light

Crypto firms are running out of time for President Donald Trump and Congress to sign off on long-awaited legislation defining how the industry will be regulated. The outcome could have big implications for trading platforms like Coinbase Global and for the direction of token prices in coming months.

On Thursday, groups representing crypto firms and banks are expected to meet with the White House in an attempt to resolve the latest roadblock. The problem is that some senators and banks want to ensure that any bill prohibits companies from paying yield to customers for holding stablecoins on their platforms.

The provision is important to Coinbase, which co-created the largest U.S.-based stablecoin, a type of token that is pegged to the dollar. Some banks and lawmakers say offering the yields could cause customers to pull out deposits and move the money into the tokens.

The conflict dominated discussions at a crypto conference held on Wednesday at Trump's Mar-a-Lago resort. The conference was hosted by World Liberty Financial, the digital-assets firm co-founded by Trump, his sons Eric and Don Jr., and others including Zach Witkoff, the son of Steve Witkoff, the White House special envoy to the Middle East.

World Liberty offers its own stablecoin, called USD1, which has grown to have a market capitalization of more than $5 billion since its launch last year. USDC, which Coinbase helped create, has a market value of more than $73 billion.

The conference came amid a crisis of confidence in the crypto market. Bitcoin has nearly halved from last year's all-time high to about $67,000 as of Thursday. Bitcoin has faltered even as the S&P 500 has hit record highs. Gold, which some crypto proponents thought Bitcoin could replace, is 68% higher than its year-ago level.

As crypto prices have slid, speculation on the currencies has diminished as well, cutting into fee income for trading platforms, some firms have increasingly turned to stablecoins as a source of revenue. A law signed by Trump last summer requires the coins to be backed by reserves that include Treasuries and bank deposits. Stablecoin issuers earn yield on the reserves backing the tokens.

Coinbase in its earnings report last week said 20% of its revenue in the fourth quarter — or $364 million — came from stablecoins. Less than 10% came from stablecoins in the fourth quarter of 2024.

That has heightened the stakes for the battle playing out in Congress. Coinbase pays a yield of 3.5% on USDC holdings, comparable to that of government money-market funds and higher than most savings accounts. Once an investor owns a stablecoin, he or she is also one step closer to buying other cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin, which are typically bought with stablecoins.

As late as last month, lawmakers seemed poised to move forward with the bill, which would take most crypto tokens out of the purview of the Securities and Exchange Commission. That has long been an industry goal after the SEC under President Joe Biden sued most major platforms for allegedly violating securities laws. A similar bill passed the House with widespread bipartisan support last year.

However, the Senate's version of the bill ran aground after lawmakers planned to include the yield ban. Coinbase withdrew its support, and a Senate committee delayed a vote on the bill hours later.

Most banks are treating stablecoins "as an opportunity, and there's still a handful of folks that are treating it as a threat and trying to be the entrenched incumbent and curtail their competition. We have very little tolerance for that," said Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong at the conference.

Now, there is a high risk that lawmakers might not manage to approve the legislation before the November midterm elections. Passing a bill is expected to get only harder if Democrats retake the House in November.

Sen. Bernie Moreno (R., Ohio), one of the industry's biggest proponents at the Mar-a-Lago conference, said he hoped lawmakers could come to an agreement by April but warned that they were running out of time. He implored the executives in attendance to draw on "any political connections you have, especially on my Democratic colleagues, over the next 90 days."

Earlier talks between the White House, banks, and crypto firms failed to bring a compromise. An initial proposal by banking trade groups sought to ban not just offering yield for holding stablecoins but rewards for using them, with limited exemptions. There is hope among some crypto lobbyists that lawmakers will at least accept rewards for using the coins, drawing a parallel to credit card rewards.

There are more land mines even if crypto firms reach a compromise on the yield issue. Some Democratic senators say any bill needs to include a ban on Trump and his family profiting from crypto dealings while in office. Entities affiliated with Trump and his family through a holding company own about 38% of World Liberty, according to its website.

Earlier this year after a Wall Street Journal report, World Liberty acknowledged that an Abu Dhabi royal had bought nearly half of the company days before the inauguration, which Democrats have pointed to as a conflict of interest and national security threat.

At the Mar-a-Lago conference, Eric and Don Jr. told attendees that their creation of World Liberty happened in part out of necessity, after their companies were denied basic banking services. The White House has said Trump has no involvement with his companies while in office and denied there are conflicts of interest.

Write to Joe Light at joe.light@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Dollar advances after data implies stable labor market
data.description:
Key points:
  • US jobless claims fall
  • Euro declines as ECB leadership speculation swirls
  • Dollar climbs for fourth straight session

By Chuck Mikolajczak

The dollar strengthened for a fourth straight session on Thursday after data indicated the economy was on stable footing, giving the Federal Reserve leeway to hold interest rates in check in the near-term.

The Labor Department said weekly initial jobless claims fell by 23,000 to an adjusted 206,000, below the 225,000 estimate of economists polled by Reuters.

"It doesn't look like an economy suffering from higher rates. Even with the pressure from the White House to lower rates, that pressure doesn't really hit until May, so there's no real trend to this information right now," said Joseph Trevisani, senior analyst at FXStreet in New York.

"That's why we're headed back to a range. Without a reason to move, traders are going to be conservative."

TRADE DEFICIT WIDENED

Other data from the Commerce Department showed the U.S. trade deficit widened to $70.3 billion, well above the $55.5 billion estimate.

The dollar index , which measures the dollar against a basket of currencies, rose 0.19% to 97.88, with the euro down 0.14% at $1.1766. The four-day run of gains for the greenback would mark its longest streak since early January.

The euro fell for a second straight day, extending declines after its biggest daily percentage decline since January 30 on Wednesday after the Financial Times reported that European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde was expected to leave her post before the end of her eight-year term.

Four sources told Reuters that Lagarde has informed colleagues she remains focused on her job and she would tell them first if she was about to step down, a message they took to mean she was not about to resign.

Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari said the labor market has remained "pretty resilient" and that the central bank is close on both mandates of maximum employment and stable prices.

Markets are not pricing in more than a 50% chance for a rate cut of at least 25 basis points from the Fed until its June meeting, according to CME's FedWatch Tool.

Fed minutes released on Wednesday showed policymakers were divided over where to take U.S. rates and suggested that the next chairman, due to start in May, would struggle to push through rate cuts.

Investors were mindful of a reported buildup of U.S. forces in the Middle East and a potential U.S.-Iran conflict, a concern that lifted oil prices. U.S. President Donald Trump said Thursday that "really bad things will happen" if no deal is reached with Iran and the United States will get a deal one way or the other.

Against the Japanese yen , the dollar strengthened 0.06% to 154.92 while sterling weakened 0.25% to $1.3457.

Bank of England interest rate-setter Catherine Mann said British inflation data published this week represented "good numbers" although there was not as much improvement in the underlying figures as the central bank had hoped to see.

data.title: Seven & i Targets A$400 Million Australia EBITDA by 2030
data.description:
Seven & i Holdings (SVNDY) is positioning Australia as a real-time test case for its next phase of growth, betting that the Japan-style 7-Eleven model built around fresh food and daily essentials could resonate beyond its home market. After acquiring the Australian operator for A$1.7 billion ($1.2 billion) in 2024, management is targeting a sharp acceleration in store openings, aiming for 1,000 locations by 2030. Chairman Shin Abe has described Australia as a proving ground to refine a winning formula that, if successful, could support broader expansion toward 30 countries this decade. The strategic push comes as the company continues restructuring efforts that intensified after a 6.77 trillion ($43.2 billion) takeover proposal from Alimentation Couche-Tard in 2024, even though that offer was later withdrawn.

Operationally, the shift is tangible. Since taking direct control, the Australian unit has nearly doubled its product range to around 3,000 items, leaning into hot and cold prepared foods, snacks, drinks and groceries. According to local CEO Fiona Hayes, revenue across those food and beverage categories each increased 14% to 15% in 2025, with similar growth trends continuing this year. Hayes has said that lost tobacco sales, pressured by tighter regulation and higher cigarette taxes, have already been offset. More than 150 of the country's 763 stores have undergone substantial refurbishments, including installation of automated ovens and fryers, as the company works toward a goal of roughly doubling Australian EBITDA to A$400 million by 2030.

That said, management has acknowledged that execution remains critical. Some items, such as a A$16 pepperoni pizza, have struggled to gain traction, and replicating the texture of onigiri has proven challenging under local food storage regulations. Rising interest rates in Australia are again weighing on consumer sentiment, adding another variable to the rollout. Analysts at Bloomberg Intelligence have noted that lower population density, higher price sensitivity and strong competition from Woolworths Group and Coles Group could complicate the repositioning of 7-Eleven from a fuel-focused stop to a credible food-led convenience destination. If the model scales, it could validate Seven & i's move toward greater direct ownership and global expansion; if not, it may raise questions about how transferable the Japanese playbook truly is.

data.title: Cocoa futures extend slide, coffee prices also fall
data.description:
London cocoa futures on ICE sank to their lowest in more than 2-1/2 years on Thursday as concerns about unsold stocks of cocoa in both Ivory Coast and Ghana continued to weigh on prices, while coffee prices also fell.

COCOA

* London cocoa settled down 162 pounds, or 7%, to 2,146 pounds per metric ton after setting a more than 2-1/2-year low of 2,133 pounds.

* Dealers said producer selling, particularly from Ivory Coast, had contributed to the sharp fall in prices.

* They noted weak global demand has led to a build-up in stocks in West Africa and helped to drive down prices.

* Ghana has already cut the price paid to farmers in a bid to boost sales, while Ivory Coast is considering following suit.

* New York cocoa lost 7.7% to $3,058 a ton, having hit a more than 2-1/2-year low of $3,052.

COFFEE

* Robusta coffee settled down $63, or 1.7%, at $3,620 a ton, after hitting a six-month low of $3,544 per ton earlier.

* Arabica coffee was little changed at $2.854 per lb, having earlier hit the lowest price since November 2024 at $2.7565 per pound.

* Dealers said abundant rainfall had encouraged the growth of coffee cherries in Brazil and the outlook for this year's harvest was favourable.

* The sharp drop in prices has, however, brought selling by farmers in Brazil to a virtual standstill.

SUGAR

* Raw sugar ​settled down 0.1 cent, or 0.7%, at 14.07 cents per lb. The market slumped to a five-year low of 13.67 cents last week.

* Dealers said supplies remained ample with a global surplus widely forecast for the current 2025/26 season.

* White sugar lost 1.1% to $403.30 a ton.

data.title: Canadian dollar steadies near 13-day low as trade deficit narrows
data.description:
Key points:
  • Loonie touches its weakest since February 6 at 1.3714
  • Trade deficit narrows to C$1.31 billion in December
  • Price of oil settles 1.9% higher
  • Bond yields were little changed across the curve

By Fergal Smith

The Canadian dollar steadied after six straight days of declines against its U.S. counterpart on Thursday as oil prices rose and data showed that Canada's trade deficit narrowed in December.

The loonie was trading nearly unchanged at 1.37 per U.S. dollar, or 72.99 U.S. cents, after earlier touching its weakest level since February 6 at 1.3714.

Canada's trade deficit narrowed to C$1.31 billion ($957 million) in December from a revised C$2.59 billion in November, as a jump in exports of unwrought gold helped lift total exports by 2.6%.

For 2025, exports edged down 0.2%, weighed by reduced exports to the United States after it began a trade war.

"Canada has thus far escaped the worst of the trade blow," Karl Schamotta, chief market strategist at Corpay, said in a note. "But officials on both sides of the border are preparing for acrimonious negotiations in the months ahead - and we think (currency) hedgers should be doing the same."

The United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement, which has shielded much of Canada's exports from U.S. tariffs, is set for review by a July 1 deadline.

The price of oil , one of Canada's major exports, settled 1.9% higher at $66.43 a barrel as traders worried about escalating tensions between the United States and Iran, which have stepped up military activity in the oil-producing Middle East.

The safe-haven U.S. dollar added to recent gains against a basket of major currencies after U.S. data indicated the economy was stable, giving the Federal Reserve leeway to hold interest rates in check.

Canadian bond yields were little changed across the curve. The 10-year (CA10YT=RR) was down less than half a basis point at 3.226%, after touching on Tuesday a near seven-week low at 3.204%.

data.title: Fintech Clear Street withdraws US IPO filing
data.description:
Wall Street broker Clear Street has withdrawn its registration statement for a U.S. listing, the company said on Thursday, as heightened bearish sentiments on financial and software stocks now extend to the new listings market.

Last week, Clear Street had delayed its IPO plans, citing "market conditions". This had followed a massive slashing of its targeted raise.

Disruption fears from AI-first offerings have been fueling broad market selloffs, with shares of Wall Street brokerages hit last week after a selloff in software and IT stocks earlier this month.

data.title: Key facts: Moderna shares rise on flu vaccine review; BioNTech sues Moderna; Patent case setback for Moderna
data.description:
• Moderna's shares jumped 5.9% after the FDA agreed to review its seasonal flu vaccine, mRNA-1010. A decision is expected by August 5, 2026, for adults 50 and older.12
  • BioNTech has sued Moderna in Delaware, claiming that its COVID-19 vaccine, mNEXSPIKE, infringes on a patent linked to BioNTech and Pfizer's Comirnaty vaccine.3
  • Moderna's patent case against Arbutus Biopharma faced a setback as a judge dismissed two defenses. However, Moderna can still argue that Arbutus did not fully disclose its invention.4

data.title: Key facts: Microsoft stock dips post-Q4; board member buys $2M shares; Azure data storage surges
data.description:
• Microsoft's stock trades at a forward P/E ratio of 22.1x, lower than Alphabet's 26.0x. After a weak Q4 report, Microsoft lost $357 billion in market cap.1
  • Microsoft board member John Stanton bought 5,000 MSFT shares for about $2 million at $397.35 each, the largest insider purchase in 11 years, raising his total to 83,905 shares.234
  • Microsoft's Azure cloud platform experienced a surge in data storage, with ICE tripling its data in six months. Microsoft provides cloud tools to ICE but prohibits mass surveillance.56

data.title: Key facts: Meta Q4 revenues up 23.8% to $59.89B; stock falls 4.2%
data.description:
• Meta Platforms posted Q4 revenues of $59.89 billion, up 23.8% year-on-year, surpassing forecasts. Despite this, the stock fell 4.2% to $640.50 post-report.1
  • Meta Platforms is in a California trial over its apps' mental health impact on teens. CEO Mark Zuckerberg testified, emphasizing the company's focus on useful content and denying addiction claims.23
  • Meta Platforms plans to invest up to $135 billion in AI data centers, emphasizing its commitment to artificial intelligence, despite lacking a cloud unit similar to competitors like Amazon and Google.4

data.title: Key facts: Lemonade Q4 Revenue Up 53.3% to $228.1M; Shares Surge 18%
data.description:
• Lemonade (LMND) reported Q4 revenue of $228.1 million, up 53.3% year-on-year. Net premiums earned reached $179.5 million, a 77.4% increase. Shares rose nearly 18% in pre-market trading.12
  • Lemonade saw growth from AI-driven pricing models, boosting revenue and profits. Diversified expansion in Pet, Car, and European markets enhanced underwriting performance.3

data.title: Key facts: JPMorgan Chase fined €12.18M for misreporting; $5B lawsuit shift
data.description:
• JPMorgan Chase's European unit was fined €12.18 million for misreporting capital requirements due to incorrect risk-weighted asset calculations from 2019 to 2024. The bank acknowledged the fine.1
  • JPMorgan Chase is moving to transfer a $5 billion lawsuit to federal court, claiming former President Trump fraudulently included CEO Jamie Dimon to avoid federal jurisdiction.2

data.title: Key facts: Goldman Sachs Q4 revenues fall 3% to $13.45 billion; focuses on crypto tech
data.description:
• Goldman Sachs reported Q4 revenues of $13.45 billion, down 3% year-on-year, but beat forecasts by 0.5%. Its stock price is flat, currently at $933.01.1
  • Goldman Sachs is prioritizing cryptocurrency technology, especially tokenization and stablecoins, instead of investing heavily in cryptocurrencies, according to CEO David Solomon.2

data.title: Key facts: Germany to acquire 35 more F-35 jets; Lockheed to supply C-130J devices
data.description:
• Germany plans to buy over 35 more F-35 jets from Lockheed Martin, boosting its fleet to about 85. Lockheed is focused on current orders amid challenges in Germany's joint fighter program.12
  • Lockheed Martin plans to deliver advanced C-130J training devices to the Royal Australian Air Force, with deliveries scheduled to commence in 2029.3

data.title: Key facts: American Water Works reports $5.14B revenue; repays $795M note
data.description:
• American Water Works Company (AWK) reported 2025 revenues of $5.14 billion and net income of $1.11 billion. For 2026, it projects adjusted EPS of $6.02 to $6.12.123
  • On February 13, 2026, American Water Works repaid a $795 million secured note. Shareholders also approved merger proposals with Essential Utilities, boosting the company's growth potential.4

data.title: Key facts: Amazon surpasses Walmart in sales; AWS growth hits 24%; 2026 capex projected at $200B
data.description:
• Amazon (AMZN) reported $716.9 billion in net sales for fiscal year 2025, surpassing Walmart's $713.2 billion, marking a 12% growth for Amazon and ending Walmart's 13-year revenue lead.12
  • AWS growth hit 24% last quarter, the fastest in 13 quarters, with rising capital spending seen as a positive sign for the cloud sector, potentially exceeding market growth expectations.3
  • Amazon.com projected its 2026 capital expenditures to be around $200 billion, emphasizing its substantial investment in AI data centers.4

data.title: Key facts: Altria Group shifts to smoke-free products; strong Marlboro sales
data.description:
• Altria Group is concentrating on a transition to smoke-free products, highlighting significant growth in its oral tobacco and nicotine pouch segments.1
  • Altria Group's CFO reported strong Marlboro sales, indicating resilience in premium cigarette brands despite consumers cutting back on spending due to inflation.2

data.title: Key facts: AMD stock rises; Crusoe invests $400M in chips; $1B for World Labs
data.description:
• AMD stock rose nearly 2% as ARK Invest, led by Cathie Wood, bought about 30,139 shares worth $6.12 million, boosting trading activity.1
  • Crusoe is installing AMD chips in a new data center in Ohio, with plans to purchase $400 million worth of AMD's new chips as it shifts focus from cryptocurrency to AI infrastructure.2
  • Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) joined a $1 billion funding round for World Labs, a startup focused on spatial intelligence technology, alongside major investors like Nvidia and Autodesk.3

data.title: B3 Consulting Group: Revenue up 7.1% in 2025, EBIT margin improved, but net income fell due to prior one-off gain
data.description:
Revenue grew 7.1% in 2025, driven by acquisitions, but organic growth was negative. EBIT margin improved to 4.0% and cash flow strengthened, while net income fell due to a prior year one-off gain. No dividend is proposed, and the company remains focused on growth and integration.

Original document: B3 Consulting Group AB [B3] Interim report — Feb. 20 2026

Disclaimer

data.title: Chadian women take centre stage in Berlinale competition entry 'Soumsoum'
data.description:
Key points:
  • Film set in Chadian desert amid ongoing internal conflicts
  • Director Haroun previously won jury prizes at Cannes and Venice
  • Newcomer Maimouna Miawama stars as mystic teenager

By Swantje Stein

Chadian filmmaker Mahamat‑Saleh Haroun said on Thursday that his new Berlin Film Festival competition entry "Soumsoum, the Night of the Stars" stems from a desire to highlight the women he believes are central to Chad's social fabric.

"They are those who transmit the memory of a lot of things," he told Reuters ahead of the premiere.

"And in a country like Chad, where we have civil war for decades, I think that tenderness and friendship is a kind of resistance to the violence," added the director who has previously won jury prizes at the Cannes and Venice festivals.

The landlocked Central African country has experienced a nearly continuous series of civil wars and internal conflicts since it gained its independence from France in 1960.

The film follows 17-year-old Kellou, played by newcomer Maimouna Miawama, who grapples with understanding newfound supernatural powers that make her unwelcome in the village.

She forms a bond with Aya, played by Achouackh Abakar Souleymane, an older outcast striving to preserve mystical and cultural traditions as her own time comes to a close.

THE STRENGTH OF WOMEN

Miawama said stepping into her first lead role had been deeply emotional.

"I'm very proud of myself, but it was also challenging, as I had to focus more on expressing emotions through my face than delivering dialogue," she said.

The film's message about the strength of women resonated with her, and she hopes female viewers walk out feeling empowered.

"I hope that by looking at this, they can step out of their comfort zone, stop saying 'I'm inferior' and be able to achieve great things, face challenges," she said.

Souleymane, who starred in Haroun's previous feature "Lingui, The Sacred Bonds", emphasised the resilience of women.

"We have this duty to be strong and courageous, but also to ensure that good things are passed on because we are the ones raising children," she said.

"Soumsoum" is competing against 21 other films for the festival's Golden Bear top award that will be handed out on February 21.

data.title: Why eBay Stock Is Popping Today
data.description:
Shares of eBay surged about 8% in early Thursday trading after the company posted stronger-than-expected fourth-quarter results, lifted its dividend, and set out an upbeat outlook.

The e-commerce group reported Q4 net revenue of about $3 billion, up roughly 15% year over year, exceeding market expectations. Adjusted earnings per share came in at $1.41, topping consensus estimates.

Gross merchandise volume rose to $21.2 billion, reflecting stronger holiday demand, including gains from live-shopping initiatives and AI-driven tools.

Separately, eBay said it agreed to buy secondhand apparel platform Depop from Etsy for $1.2 billion in cash, a move aimed at expanding its reach among younger shoppers. The deal is expected to close later this year, subject to customary conditions.

eBay also raised its quarterly dividend to $0.31 a share from $0.29 and authorized a $2 billion share buyback.

For the first quarter, the company forecast revenue of $3.0 billion to $3.05 billion and non-GAAP EPS of $1.53 to $1.59, both above analyst estimates.

Net income slipped year over year due to higher operating costs, but management said momentum across key initiatives remains intact.

data.title: Hims & Hers to Buy Australian Telehealth Provider Eucalyptus in $1.15 Billion Deal — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Mackenzie Tatananni

Shares of Hims & Hers Health popped Thursday after the telehealth provider agreed to acquire Eucalyptus, an Australian digital health company, in a $1.15 billion deal.

The value of the deal is subject to customary purchase price adjustments and around $240 million will be payable in cash upon closing, Hims said in a press release. The remaining consideration includes guaranteed deferred payments over the subsequent 18 months and other payments tied to the achievement of financial targets through early 2029.

Hims added that it's currently prepared to finance most of the transaction with existing cash on hand as well as future operating cash flows from its U.S. operations. The deal is expected to close in the middle of the year.

The online pharmacy styled the acquisition as a way for Hims to expand its footprint globally, specifically by pushing into markets in Australia and Japan while building its presence in Canada, Germany, and the United Kingdom.

The language is familiar. In June 2025, the company announced its acquisition of ZAVA, a digital health platform based in Europe, saying the deal would expand its reach in the U.K. and officially introduce Hims into France, Germany, and Ireland.

Hims & Hers stock declined 1% after rising earlier in the session. The benchmark S&P 500 fell 0.6%.

Coming into Thursday, shares had slumped 51% in 2026 due to a maelstrom of regulatory scrutiny, concerns over decelerating growth, and, most recently, a lawsuit with Novo Nordisk, which accuses the online pharmacy of selling compounded versions of its weight-loss drugs that infringe patents.

Hims "unlawfully mass markets unapproved versions of Novo Nordisk's FDA-approved semaglutide medicines, deceiving patients and putting their health at risk," Novo said in a statement earlier this month.

Hims hit back at the drugmaker, calling the lawsuit "a blatant attack by a Danish company on millions of Americans who rely on compounded medications for access to personalized care."

Hims operates and partners with compounding pharmacies, which manufacture personalized, non-FDA-approved versions of medications. The company takes advantage of a legal framework that allows it to circumvent Food and Drug Administration approval for the products sold through its website.

So-called compounders came sharply into focus amid a shortage for weight-loss drugs that stretched into early 2025. The FDA has acknowledged that patients "may look to compounded drugs as an option to get the medication they need" during a shortage.

While the FDA deemed the shortage resolved last year and ordered compounders to halt production of drugs that were "essentially a copy" of FDA-approved products, Hims argued that the pill was an adjusted dosage, making it markedly different from Novo's product.

However, Hims said in early February that it would stop selling its $49 weight-loss pill containing semaglutide, the active ingredient in Novo's Wegovy.

The company continues to sell compounded GLP-1 injections, and focuses heavily on marketing these products. Hims stock began rising sharply at the start of 2025 as the company moved to capitalize on the demand for weight-loss drugs by selling copycat versions of FDA-approved products online.

Write to Mackenzie Tatananni at mackenzie.tatananni@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Invitation Homes Inc. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Invitation Homes Inc., a leading owner and operator of single-family rental homes in the United States, has released its 2025 10-K report, showcasing robust financial performance, strategic growth initiatives, and a comprehensive overview of the challenges and risks faced by the company. The report highlights the company's commitment to enhancing shareholder value through strategic acquisitions, operational excellence, and a resident-centric business model.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Revenues: $2,729.3 million, reflecting a 4.2% increase from the previous year, driven by higher rental revenues and management fee revenues.
  • Net Income: $589.9 million, a 29.5% increase from the prior year, attributed to improved operational profitability and reduced expenses related to litigation and regulatory matters.
  • Net Income Per Common Share — Diluted: $0.96, showing an increase from the previous year, indicating improved earnings performance.
  • FFO (Funds From Operations): $1,108.8 million, highlighting the company's ability to generate cash flow from its real estate operations.
  • Core FFO: $1,176.8 million, reflecting adjustments for non-cash interest expenses and other non-recurring items, providing a clearer picture of recurring cash flow.

Business Highlights

  • Geographical Performance: Invitation Homes operates in 16 core markets across the United States, with a strong presence in the Western United States, Florida, and the Southeast United States. The Western United States accounts for 38.8% of revenue, with Southern California, Northern California, and Seattle showing high occupancy rates above 95%. Florida contributes 32.4% of revenue, with South Florida and Tampa being key markets. The Southeast United States, including Atlanta and the Carolinas, represents 18.7% of revenue.
  • Sales Units: As of December 31, 2025, Invitation Homes wholly owns 86,192 homes for lease and jointly owns 8,006 homes. The company also manages an additional 15,866 homes on behalf of third parties.
  • New Product Launches: In January 2026, Invitation Homes acquired ResiBuilt, a leading fee homebuilder specializing in single-family rental communities, enhancing its platform with home building capabilities.
  • New Production Launches: The acquisition of ResiBuilt adds land development and construction expertise, allowing Invitation Homes to develop single-family rental homes and communities internally across high-growth markets.
  • Future Outlook: Invitation Homes plans to leverage its acquisition of ResiBuilt to support long-term growth by providing a reliable pipeline of purpose-built rental homes. The company aims to enhance operational control, cost efficiency, and execution on growth opportunities in strategically important markets.
  • Operational Strategy: The company employs a vertically integrated operating platform to acquire, renovate, lease, maintain, and manage homes. This approach is designed to capture local density benefits and economies of scale.
  • Resident-Centric Model: Invitation Homes emphasizes a high-touch customer service model, focusing on providing clean, safe, and functional homes, which is central to its operations across corporate headquarters and field offices.
  • Sustainability and Corporate Responsibility: The company is committed to sustainability and corporate responsibility, focusing on creating a better living environment for residents and supporting community vitality.
  • Impact of Macroeconomic Trends: The company is navigating challenges such as inflation, interest rate volatility, and regulatory changes affecting institutional ownership of single-family homes, which could impact its operations and growth strategies.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Strategic Initiatives: Invitation Homes acquired ResiBuilt, a leading fee homebuilder specializing in single-family rental communities, to enhance its growth strategy by adding home building capabilities. This acquisition supports the company's long-term growth by providing a reliable pipeline of purpose-built rental homes. Additionally, the company is focused on strategic mergers and acquisitions to capture operating benefits and economies of scale in high-demand markets.
  • Capital Management: The company authorized a Share Repurchase Program allowing for the acquisition of up to $500 million of its common stock. During 2025, Invitation Homes repurchased 2,232,685 shares for $61.3 million. The company also issued $600 million in 4.95% Senior Notes maturing in 2033 and entered into new interest rate swap agreements to manage interest rate risk. Dividends were paid quarterly, with a total of $715.4 million distributed in 2025, reflecting the company's commitment to returning capital to shareholders.
  • Future Outlook: Invitation Homes plans to maintain or improve its credit ratings and aims to be a predominantly unsecured borrower with a target net debt of approximately 5.5 to 6.0 times trailing twelve months Adjusted EBITDAre. The company intends to refinance a significant portion of its secured debt maturing in 2027 with unsecured debt. It will continue to focus on strategic acquisitions and development opportunities to support its growth strategy, while navigating macroeconomic challenges such as inflation and interest rate volatility.

Challenges and Risks

  • Economic and Market Risks: The company faces significant risks related to its business environment and industry, particularly in the ownership and rental of residential real estate. Key risks include fluctuating economic conditions, changes in job markets, and declines in residential real estate values. Additionally, legislative actions aimed at limiting institutional ownership of single-family homes could impact the company's ability to acquire properties and impose additional compliance requirements.
  • Operational Risks: The single-family rental market is subject to various factors that could affect rental income, such as macroeconomic shifts, competition, and regulatory changes. Inflation and other macroeconomic factors, including interest rate volatility, could adversely affect the company's financial results by increasing operating costs and impacting consumer spending. The company is also exposed to risks from increasing property taxes, insurance costs, and HOA fees, which could negatively impact financial results if rental rates cannot be increased to offset these costs. Furthermore, pandemics and outbreaks of infectious diseases pose a risk to business operations, potentially affecting rental revenues and the ability to make debt service payments.
  • Management and Operational Challenges: Operational risks include challenges in managing growth, as the company has rapidly expanded its portfolio. The ability to effectively manage properties and maintain resident satisfaction is crucial. Fixed costs and expenses may not decrease with revenue reductions, posing a risk to financial stability. The company has recorded net losses in the past and may continue to do so due to significant non-cash charges. Dependence on key executive officers and associates, along with competition for skilled personnel, presents a risk to operations. Labor shortages and rising labor costs could further impact financial performance.
  • Concentration and Market Risks: The company's investments are concentrated in specific markets and the single-family properties sector, exposing it to seasonal fluctuations and economic downturns. Renovation and maintenance costs may be higher than anticipated, affecting operating results.
  • Legal and Regulatory Risks: Executive actions and proposed legislation aimed at limiting institutional ownership of single-family homes could materially affect the company's business and growth strategy. Compliance with existing and potential new regulations may result in significant costs and delays, impacting the company's ability to acquire, renovate, or dispose of properties. Eviction, tenant rights, rent control, and rent stabilization laws may limit the company's ability to collect rent and enforce lease terms, negatively impacting rental income and profitability. The company is also subject to regulatory proceedings and litigation, which could result in significant expenses and reputational harm.
  • Information Technology and Cybersecurity Risks: The company is highly dependent on information systems, and system failures could disrupt business operations. The use of third-party service providers and digital marketing platforms exposes the company to risks outside its control, including changes in pricing and service disruptions. The company faces risks from leasing fraud, which could result in lost revenue and increased expenses. Additionally, eminent domain could lead to material losses on property investments.

SEC Filing: Invitation Homes Inc. [ INVH ] - 10-K - Feb. 19, 2026

data.title: Figma Targets 30% Revenue Growth In 2026
data.description:
Figma is heading into 2026 with confidence, targeting 30% revenue growth as AI tools drive deeper engagement across its platform. The company just wrapped what CEO Dylan Field called its best quarter yet, and the numbers back that up.

Q4 revenue came in at $304 million, up 40% year over year and above the high end of guidance. For the full year, revenue reached $1.056 billion. Net dollar retention for customers spending more than $10 thousand in ARR climbed to 136%, up 5 percentage points sequentially. Gross margin held strong at 86% in Q4, and operating income reached $44 million, or a 14% margin. Figma ended the year with $1.7 billion in cash. AI adoption is accelerating fast, with weekly active users of Figma Make jumping more than 70% quarter over quarter, and over half of large customers now using it weekly.

Looking ahead, management expects 2026 revenue between $1.366 billion and $1.374 billion and non GAAP operating income of $100 million to $110 million.

data.title: Merrill Accuses Dynasty of Reneging on Agreement to Arbitrate Dispute Over Giant Breakaway Team — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Andrew Welsch

Months after Merrill Lynch sued a $129 billion financial advisor team that left to launch an independent firm, Merrill finds itself back in federal court, this time accusing one of the defendants, Dynasty Financial Partners, of reneging on an agreement to arbitrate their dispute.

In a Feb. 12 filing, Merrill says that Dynasty, alongside the advisors and Charles Schwab, agreed to arbitrate its claims that they conspired to "poach" its business. But in the months since then, Dynasty has taken the position that it never consented to arbitration at Finra, the brokerage industry's self-regulatory organization, and that it wouldn't submit to Finra's jurisdiction, according to Merrill.

"Merrill now finds itself at a procedural impasse," the company's filing says. "Dynasty, despite its conduct during the TRO [temporary restraining order] hearing and thereafter, refuses to arbitrate, and the court action is stayed."

Merrill is asking a judge to either compel Dynasty to arbitrate the case or allow it to pursue claims against Dynasty in court while it proceeds with its arbitration case against the advisors. The latter option "is the most equitable given Finra's lack of jurisdiction over Dynasty and Dynasty's apparent (and belated) refusal to consent to Finra arbitration, " according to Merrill's filing.

A spokeswoman for Dynasty says the company "strenuously disagrees with Merrill's continuing mischaracterizations at every point in this matter and we are preparing our response to this most recent attack."

St. Petersburg, Fla.-based Dynasty helps advisor teams leave national brokerage firms, such as Merrill, to open registered investment advisory firms. It also provides technology, asset management, and other services to RIAs. When the former Merrill advisor team quit in September to launch OpenArc Corporate Advisory in Atlanta, it did so with help from Dynasty, which also took a minority equity stake in OpenArc, according to a press release.

OpenArc uses Charles Schwab as its custodian, meaning that Schwab provides OpenArc with a trading platform and safeguards client assets on its behalf. A Schwab spokeswoman declined to comment on the case.

Merrill filed its lawsuit in a federal court in Atlanta against the advisors, Dynasty, and Schwab on the same day that the advisors quit. In October, a federal judge denied the company's request for a temporary restraining order.

Write to Andrew Welsch at andrew.welsch@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
